---
name: settings-table
description: "Add or refactor a reorderable, editable settings table in a Dot.vu component using TableContainer, drag-and-drop, and either a single inline delete button (when delete is the only action) or OptionsMenuRootButton with Edit, Duplicate, Delete (when multiple actions are needed). Use when adding a dynamic settings list to editor.js and live.js."
---

# Settings Table Pattern

Use this skill when adding or refactoring an editable settings table in a Dot.vu component.

Do not invent new patterns. Reuse existing patterns from the current component when possible.

Read first: [dotvu-component.instructions.md](../../instructions/dotvu-component.instructions.md), [dotvu-api skill](../dotvu-api/SKILL.md), and the three runtime files.

---

## Requirements

For every editable list or array setting:

- Store items as an array of objects.
- Every item must have an `id` generated by `getUniqueId()`.
- Use `TableContainer` to display the list.
- Use the first compact column as a drag handle when item order affects rendering.
- Use the last compact column for row actions.
  - When multiple actions are needed (Edit, Duplicate, Delete), use `OptionsMenuRootButton`.
  - When delete is the only action, use a single inline `<button>` with the trash SVG (see **Single-action alternative** below). Do not import `IconButton` or anything from `@icons` for this case.
- Include Add behavior through `TableContainer onAdd`.
- Edit item details inside `Drawer` and `DrawerSection`.
- Save every input change immediately.
- Do not add Save or Cancel buttons.
- Preserve existing state values and saved data.

---

## Nested list requirements

If an item contains its own editable child array (e.g. bullets, options, answers, cards, sub-items):

- Keep the child array inside the parent item.
- Every child item must have an `id` generated by `getUniqueId()`.
- Use a nested `TableContainer` inside the parent item's `DrawerSection`.
- Child rows must support Duplicate and Delete.
- Child rows should support drag-and-drop reorder when order affects rendering.
- Duplicating a parent item must regenerate IDs for both the parent and all nested child items.
- Do not flatten nested data unless explicitly requested.

---

## Standard editor state

Use local React state in `Settings` for drawer and drag UI:

```js
const [drawerOpen, setDrawerOpen] = useState(false);
const [editingId, setEditingId] = useState(null);
const [draggedId, setDraggedId] = useState(null);
const [dropTargetId, setDropTargetId] = useState(null);
```

For nested lists:

```js
const [draggedChildId, setDraggedChildId] = useState(null);
const [dropTargetChildId, setDropTargetChildId] = useState(null);
```

Clear both drag and drop-target state in the matching `handleDragEnd` helper and after a successful drop so the highlight does not linger.

---

## Standard operations

Implement these helpers (adapt naming to your domain):

- `handleAddItem`
- `handleEditItem`
- `updateEditingItem`
- `handleDuplicateItem`
- `handleDeleteItem`
- `moveItem`
- `handleDragStart`
- `handleDragOver`
- `handleDrop`
- `handleDragEnd`

For nested lists:

- `handleAddChildItem`
- `updateChildItem`
- `handleDuplicateChildItem`
- `handleDeleteChildItem`
- `moveChildItem`

When you use `handleDragStart`, `handleDragOver`, and `handleDrop`, prefer curried helpers that accept the item id and return the event handler. This keeps the row JSX compact and makes it easier to attach the same handlers to both the drag handle and the summary cell.

---

## Duplicate behavior

When duplicating an item:

- Insert the duplicate immediately after the source item.
- Generate a new `id` for the duplicate.
- If the item has nested arrays, generate new IDs for all child items.
- Preserve all values except IDs.

```js
function duplicateItemWithNewIds(item) {
  return {
    ...item,
    id: getUniqueId(),
    children: Array.isArray(item.children)
      ? item.children.map((child) => ({
          ...child,
          id: getUniqueId(),
        }))
      : [],
  };
}
```

Rename `children` to match your actual field (e.g. `bullets`, `options`, `cards`).

---

## UI expectations

- Keep the table easy to scan. Show summary values in table cells by default.
- Prefer a single primary summary column such as `title` to identify the item.
- Put complex fields inside the Drawer.
- Clicking `Edit` must open the item `Drawer`.
- Use `TextInput` for short text. Use `textarea` only when multi-line is explicitly needed.
- Do not edit complex objects directly inside table cells.
- Ask before replacing an existing inline edit control when it may be intentionally useful, such as a row-level `Checkbox` or `ColorPicker`.
- A small inline control can remain in the table when it is a high-value quick toggle, such as a row-level `Checkbox` for visibility or CTA enablement.
- If you keep an inline control, keep the column compact and reserve it for simple single-value edits only.

Row actions must use `OptionsMenuRootButton` with icons when multiple actions are needed:

- `editIcon`, `duplicateIcon`, `deleteIcon`
- Optional: `arrowUpIcon`, `arrowDownIcon`

Do not import icons in `live.js`.

**Single-action alternative:** When delete is the only row action needed, skip `OptionsMenuRootButton` entirely and use an inline `<button>` with the trash SVG directly. This avoids importing from `@icons` and keeps the row compact:

```jsx
<button
  key={`delete-${item.id}`}
  style={{
    display: "inline-flex",
    alignItems: "center",
    justifyContent: "center",
    width: 28,
    height: 28,
    paddingRight: 8,
    border: "none",
    borderRadius: 6,
    color: "#646464",
    cursor: "pointer",
    flexShrink: 0,
  }}
  title="Delete item"
  type="button"
  onClick={() => handleDeleteItem(item.id)}
>
  <svg
    fill="currentColor"
    height="16px"
    style={{ pointerEvents: "none" }}
    viewBox="0 -960 960 960"
    width="16px"
    xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
  >
    <path d="M292.31-140q-29.92 0-51.12-21.19Q220-182.39 220-212.31V-720h-40v-60h180v-35.38h240V-780h180v60h-40v507.69Q740-182 719-161q-21 21-51.31 21H292.31ZM680-720H280v507.69q0 5.39 3.46 8.85t8.85 3.46h375.38q4.62 0 8.46-3.85 3.85-3.84 3.85-8.46V-720ZM376.16-280h59.99v-360h-59.99v360Zm147.69 0h59.99v-360h-59.99v360ZM280-720v520-520Z" />
  </svg>
</button>
```

Use `fill="currentColor"` so the icon inherits the button's `color` value. Do not set a red background — use a neutral `color: '#646464'` that shifts on hover via CSS if needed. When using this pattern, do **not** import `IconButton`, `OptionsMenuRootButton`, or any icon from `@icons`.

---

## Drag handle expectations

- The first compact column should use a clear drag affordance, not plain punctuation characters.
- Wrap the drag handle in a small container with left padding when the handle needs breathing room from the table edge, for example `style={{ paddingLeft: 12 }}`.
- Prefer a compact button-style handle with a white background, light gray border, rounded corners, and a dark grip icon.
- On drag start, set `event.dataTransfer.effectAllowed = 'move'` and store the item id with `event.dataTransfer.setData('text/plain', itemId)`.
- On drag over, call `event.preventDefault()` and set `event.dataTransfer.dropEffect = 'move'`.
- On drop, prefer `event.dataTransfer.getData('text/plain')` and fall back to local dragged id state.
- Apply drag-over styling to the primary summary cell so the drop target is obvious.
- A good default is to attach `onDragOver` and `onDrop` to both the drag handle and the primary summary cell.
- Keep a dedicated `dropTargetId` state for the active row and use it to toggle an `is-drop-target` class on the summary cell.
- A good default handle style is:

```js
<div key={`drag-${item.id}`} style={{ paddingLeft: 12 }}>
  <button
    draggable
    className="component-drag-handle"
    title="Drag to reorder"
    type="button"
    onDragEnd={handleDragEnd}
    onDragOver={handleDragOver(item.id)}
    onDragStart={handleDragStart(item.id)}
    onDrop={handleDrop(item.id)}
  >
    <svg
      fill="#111827"
      height="24px"
      viewBox="0 -960 960 960"
      width="24px"
      xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
    >
      <path d="M360-160q-33 0-56.5-23.5T280-240q0-33 23.5-56.5T360-320q33 0 56.5 23.5T440-240q0 33-23.5 56.5T360-160Zm240 0q-33 0-56.5-23.5T520-240q0-33 23.5-56.5T600-320q33 0 56.5 23.5T680-240q0 33-23.5 56.5T600-160ZM360-400q-33 0-56.5-23.5T280-480q0-33 23.5-56.5T360-560q33 0 56.5 23.5T440-480q0 33-23.5 56.5T360-400Zm240 0q-33 0-56.5-23.5T520-480q0-33 23.5-56.5T600-560q33 0 56.5 23.5T680-480q0 33-23.5 56.5T600-400ZM360-640q-33 0-56.5-23.5T280-720q0-33 23.5-56.5T360-800q33 0 56.5 23.5T440-720q0 33-23.5 56.5T360-640Zm240 0q-33 0-56.5-23.5T520-720q0-33 23.5-56.5T600-800q33 0 56.5 23.5T680-720q0 33-23.5 56.5T600-640Z" />
    </svg>
  </button>
</div>
```

- A good default handle CSS is:

```css
.component-drag-cell {
  padding-left: 12px;
}

.component-drag-handle {
  display: inline-flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  width: 28px;
  height: 28px;
  padding: 0;
  border: 1px solid #d1d5db;
  border-radius: 6px;
  background: #ffffff;
  cursor: grab;
  user-select: none;
  appearance: none;
  -webkit-appearance: none;
  box-sizing: border-box;
  flex-shrink: 0;
}

.component-drag-handle:active {
  cursor: grabbing;
}

.component-drag-handle svg {
  width: 18px;
  height: 18px;
  display: block;
  pointer-events: none;
}

.component-table-summary {
  padding: 8px 10px;
  border: 1px solid transparent;
  border-radius: 8px;
}

.component-table-summary.is-drop-target {
  border-color: #f57b37;
  background: rgba(245, 123, 55, 0.08);
}
```

- A good default summary cell shape is:

```js
<div
  className={`component-table-summary${dropTargetId === item.id ? " is-drop-target" : ""}`}
  key={`summary-${item.id}`}
  onDragOver={handleDragOver(item.id)}
  onDrop={handleDrop(item.id)}
>
  <div style={{ fontWeight: 600 }}>{item.title || "Untitled item"}</div>
</div>
```

---

## Table shape examples

**With OptionsMenuRootButton** — drag, title, quick toggle, multi-action menu:

```js
columns={[
  { content: '', compact: true },
  { content: 'Title' },
  { content: 'Show CTA', compact: true },
  { content: '', compact: true }
]}
```

**With single delete button** — drag, one or more content columns, inline delete:

```js
columns={[
  { content: '', compact: true },
  { content: 'Image', compact: true },
  { content: 'Label' },
  { content: 'Default', compact: true },
  { content: '', compact: true }
]}
```

In both shapes the first compact column is the drag handle and the last compact column is the action button. Middle columns hold content or quick-toggle controls.

---

## Drawer expectations

- Use a single `Drawer` for editing the selected item.
- Track selected item by ID (not index).
- Derive selected item from `state.items`.
- `Edit` row action sets the selected item ID and opens the drawer.
- Wrap all drawer content in `DrawerSection`.
- Close drawer by clearing selected ID.
- Do not use Save or Cancel buttons.

---

## Live rendering

- Render items in the same order as `state.items`.
- Use item IDs as React keys. Use child item IDs for nested lists.
- Wrap all pixel values in `s()`.

---

## Preserve existing behavior

- Keep existing field names when possible.
- Normalize legacy arrays into object arrays with IDs.
- Place defaults before `...state`.
- Do not remove existing actions, triggers, responsive logic, or animation unless requested.
